speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four, interview with Ellen Leviatin Kaidanow, September 20, 1995. The-- were you and Victor very close in-- in this-- in Prien? Did you stick to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his story?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had he been with--

speaker: interviewer
text: With them? Or he never talked either?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- no, we didn't know anything about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get information that you were going to America? How did they introduce that?

speaker: interviewee
text: There, actually, we knew that that was our destination. We knew that we were there just temporary. And what happened was on first come, first serve basis, when they did get, let's say, a grant, or-- or-- or-- or I don't know how it was worked, whether it was quotas. And you know, a certain amount of people. So the ones that were there before, let's say, they had a hundred going.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that hundred went. And we were next. We were there almost a full year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did someone send-- they sent an affidavit for you, from Freeport?

speaker: interviewee
text: We made out the affidavit, I believe. Or maybe they did send. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? You ?] [? had ?] [? to ?] [? come ?] [? from-- ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. But I knew that the destination was to go to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And-- and the orphanage paid for, or whoever the organization was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything. The HIAS, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have-- did you see the Greenbergs while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Maybe once or twice. But not on the steady, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember-- how did you come across? By-- by boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: By boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: General Black.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you land?

speaker: interviewee
text: I landed, I guess, that night. I don't know. Staten Island.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Staten Island?

speaker: interviewee
text: It had to be Staten Island, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or no?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, New York, Staten Island.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and who met you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was all by group. They took us to a hotel. And I don't remember the name of it. But it was someplace Downtown or the East Side. I believe it was the-- the Downtown. And, you know, they informed, in our case, this aunt who's was-- was not there. But-- and this girl, Bernice, came to-- to the place. And then her father came. And they had to check us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: They checked us out for lice and health purposes, and so on. The HIAS, before they released us. I remember they took us into stores for shoes and things like that. You know? Gave us some clothes. And then we went with her, to her house. Did you have any people with you all along, guiding you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the sense-- yes, there were, you know, people that were responsible for us. It wasn't like we were on our own.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- you know, he's here now. And for the moment, I truly forgot his name. In fact, he wrote a book. But the name escapes me at this moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was part of your--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was like, yes-- he was responsible for us, until we were settled, wherever it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first impression of Bernice and of the environment into which you were coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, I just remember crying and crying and crying. Bernice was fine. Because she took to us right away, to me especially. It's like the sister she never had. But the United-- first of all, I was very embarrassed to speak English. Because of course, it was no English at all. It was like a broken thing. I-- I suffered a lot. I remember that. It was strange.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she speak Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She was five years old. She spoke Yiddish. Yeah, she had her own life story. You know? Even in the United States. It wasn't like mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But also pretty bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your first days like in Freeport, Long Island?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bewildered, really scared, you know, not knowing what's what. I remember she'd registered me to school, to grade school. And there was a German girl that was translating for me. I barely knew German. But because I knew Jewish, it's sort of similar.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --it was very, very difficult, very difficult. It's, like, you know, you're-- it's like being scared all-- all your life, really. You know? From five years on, you're-- Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --just always scared of something. If it's not the Germans, it's new people. If it's not new people, it's new places. If it's not new places, it's new language.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know? It's-- it remains with you. I mean, you know, you fight it. But it really remains with you forever. Who was legally responsible for you in Freeport?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it's a good question. But I guess, just her father, I guess, [INAUDIBLE], yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they-- you lived with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Norman. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lived with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Until-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Until--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they bring the Greenbergs across?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they came on a quota, whenever-- however they came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they got us an apartment in the Bronx. And we all moved in together. In fact, this Bernice, who was 16, moved in with us too. Because she had, you know-- but that was for a very short time. And then we were registered to high school. They went to work. And very short. Actually, when I graduated, I met Jerry. Actually, I met him before. And we were married in 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who are your friends in the Bronx?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what high school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taft.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to Taft. So who were your friends in the Bronx?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it's funny. Because we always stuck together. We were the Three Musketeers. There were two other women. One-- women. I mean girls, at the time. One was from Russia that came here, to the United States, and lived on the concourse, which was right near us. And another one that actually was together with me in Prien. But she came here. She had nobody. So they sent her to St. Louis. She lived with her family. And then she came back to New York somehow. So I was very good friends with her. I don't think I really mingled in the sense, you know, with happiness. I was very timid, very. And sort of kept to myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any relationship with any Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with [? landsmen ?] or people that might have known your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- there was a [? landsman ?] group. I do remember going once or twice, yes. In fact, there was a man by the name of-- of [? Mendelssohn. ?] And he was very interested in bringing people together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was-- you know, he knew my parents. And but somehow, you know, they never really talked to me about it. And I never questioned it. I've never asked questions. Did you ever find any relatives?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did find, through this very [? Mendelssohn, ?] an aunt who was a sister to my mother. She came here, I believe, at like, a 19, 18, or 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know of her at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I somehow knew that they were talking about an aunt in the United States. I didn't know her last name. I didn't know where in the United States. And I just sort of dismissed it. Actually, by the time I found her, I was already married. And my son, firstborn, was three months old, to be exact. When this Mendelssohn, called me up. And he said, that he thinks, you know, he found my aunt. And it was my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when did you meet with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right after that, as soon as he called me. As I said, my son was three months old. And my husband at that time was in the United States Army. So she was, like, finding gold, better. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: A diamond.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you relate to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fantastically. We had a wonderful relationship.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. And were you able to reconstruct any kind of your former life through her?

speaker: interviewee
text: How? I don't think I could, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Family relationships?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. Because she did have-- she has-- she's not alive. But three daughters, which were very lovely people. And we were close, as long as she was around, you know. Yes, I did gain new cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: What contact did you eventually or ever have again with Lena?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very wonderful contact. I never went to see her. I never brought her here. But through somebody, I don't know through whom, we found out that she still was living in Chervonoarmiyska. And she had married somebody. At-- after, you know, at some times, during my leaving her. And in fact, it was an interesting story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because they used to write to me that Lena sleeps with a doll. Because she missed me so much, when I first left. She used to sleep with a doll. You know? In my place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway-- so we found her address. And from that time on, till the day she died, which was probably, at least, I think seven or eight years ago. Maybe more. Maybe more. We corresponded. I sent her packages. I sent her money. And we were in constant-- and I have beautiful letters from her.

speaker: interviewer
text: She is now--

speaker: interviewee
text: She--

speaker: interviewer
text: She was in the-- in-- in the Soviet Union then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, sure. Soviet Union, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: And her sister, [? Duna, ?] I have no idea of her whereabouts. But the one that we stayed with last, [? Anya, ?] lives in Lviv. And I am in touch with her now. I, again, send her money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, she wanted to come here. But we couldn't take her out. And in fact, I have a package, right now, ready to ship to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were all very wonderful to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is Anya now, about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, [SIGHS] God, how old is she? I don't know. She has to be 75--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. You have three children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much of their-- of your story have you shared with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing officially.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only sort of hearsay, or if they heard me talk. The younger-- the two older ones never ask me questions. The youngest one was more questioning. Or she would look at pictures and ask me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. At-- do you think that they did not know?

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew. They knew some-- they knew, but not in detail. I don't think they didn't. Because I never really sat down and told them exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. When do you think they-- they knew something, that your life had been different?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they always knew, of course, that I was from Europe and that I was left, you know, alone. And that my parents and sisters perished. So that, they always knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Needless to say, they knew that they never had the grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, you know-- you know, it's really ironic. Because you know, I was going to say that because of the German Nazis, I was left an orphan. Not because of natural disaster. Not because of an airplane crash. Not because of a car crash. But because of their genocide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was left an orphan. But not only was I left an orphan. But because of the German Nazis, my children never knew the love of a grandparent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in this case, unfortunately, it's on both sides. So they never-- I mean, I have grandchildren now. And I think what I give them, I really realize what my children missed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Did they ever ask questions?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I told you, they did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And again, because of the German Nazis--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I lacked education. I-- the outside facade may be good. But I really feel that inside--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, chaos. It's always been. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I tried my best because I wanted to raise my children in a normal manner. And I think that's partially why I didn't tell them. Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I didn't want them to feel--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --different or sorry for me or things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that your experiences during the Holocaust or during the war influence the way you raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I remember reading, once, a book. And it said that all the children from Holocaust survivors are not, you know-- are affected by it. And I remember being very angry about it. But in later years, you know-- see, for a very long time you don't think about it. You just put it in the back of your mind. You go to the pool. And you raise the children. And you go to the movies. And you read books. And you don't think about these things. But lately, for some reason, I just find in the last 10 years, I'm more aware of the damage that was done to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you-- I don't know if you can understand that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, I do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because many times, when I read, let's say, the children, after a hurricane, and they get all this help, psychiatric, and so on. I mean, I was walking over dead people. You know? Stepping over them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You think it doesn't affect you? I mean-- Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, let's be honest. Now, the children? Lately I'm thinking, maybe they-- I don't know how bad--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: How much they were affected.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I tried that they shouldn't be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I'm sure that if--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, would you have raised them--

speaker: interviewee
text: --talk to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: --differently? In other words, were you more protective or less protective?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that I was very much protective. And yet, I didn't want to attach myself too much to them, of fear of losing them. You know? I-- I know I never had babysitters and things like this, like I see today. You know? I never had who to leave them with. And once in awhile, if it was something very important like a wedding or a bar mitzvah, I took a babysitter. Otherwise, I was constantly with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their Jewish education or Jewish involvement, was that influenced by yours-- your experiences? Did they have--

speaker: interviewee
text: They did have. They all went-- they did not go to-- they went-- they all went to public schools. But they all went to Hebrew school as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Temple. You know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --three times a week, Sunday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And in-- have you ever gone back to Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you any interest or desire to go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't until now. But a lot of our friends have gone back. And many times at night, when I don't sleep, I sort of say to myself, gee, you know, would it be nice to retrace, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to see if it's really the way I think that it was. Because you know? So right now, it's just a thought.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we have not gone back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to believe in fate, because of everything that happened to me. Number one, that I went away with Lena and came back. And the fact that I didn't go in-- when everyone else was already in the bunk, I was still up, not you know, going in.

speaker: interviewer
text: The fact that the policeman, we were sitting in the-- in a police room. And we walked out of there, just walked out. Because this woman said, go. You know? Why are you sitting here? Go. The door is open. And he was busy. He was called away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we just walked. And I don't know, he didn't run after us or anything. So I have to say, fate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: What else could it be?

speaker: interviewer
text: What kept you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think that probably half of my life, it was just unconsciously. Like, whatever happened to me, happened. You know what I'm saying?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, I didn't plan my life. I was just there. And nobody killed me. So I was there. And I think that's pretty bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever think of yourself as Marusia or--

speaker: interviewee
text: As what?

speaker: interviewer
text: --as Marusia or as Maria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Marusia-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not-- not in that sense. I don't know. Like, sometimes I just wonder what would've happened if I stayed with her. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't think of myself as Marusia. Because I really know, consciously, that I'm not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know? For awhile, I didn't want to be. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that's over with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you would like to tell us, in terms of your story? Anything additional that we haven't touched on in that period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I told you, I think that what I said before would probably apply to your answer now. About the Germans, what they did to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --many other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And probably worse. But to me. You know? To me, just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --me myself. That it's not a life the way it should be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because as I said, it's-- it's so much easier on the outside than on the inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you-- you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. I understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, it's-- I don't know. I feel certain-- there's a sadness. You know? A void that you just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: No matter how much try to replace--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --it's somehow not there. Even though, you know, I have wonderful children, wonderful husband, thank God. And four beautiful grandchildren recently. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Still, there's something there that's not letting me enjoy it to the fullest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. What was your greatest fear from-- during this period? From the time that you were about six, or five or six, till-- I guess till you got here?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it's strange.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your greatest fear?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I cared too much. Because I remember-- when, in fact, you know, the war in our place was between the Germans and the Russians. About two weeks, I-- I read from history. Because you know, they were-- it was like, a standoff. And I remember hiding in bunkers from the bombs at this time, and laughing, not being afraid at all. It's like, I used to say to her-- you know, she would say, quiet.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, bombs are falling. I didn't care. I-- I said, so? If we die, we die. We don't-- you know? I don't-- I-- I didn't care. Mhm. Where did Lena stand on this, the Russian and German issue?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- politically, you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think they liked the Russians. But I don't remember anything about politics.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, did they like the Germans? Did they like being under German law?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I-- I really don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: She certainly wouldn't say it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: To me. Not, I don't think, that family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why you think she took you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because she was part of us. She was like-- you know? Because I think she needed us as much as we needed her, somehow. And I-- I think it was really love. You know? Like, she wanted to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why me? I don't know. You know? Sometimes I wonder.

speaker: interviewer
text: I say, you know, you could-- if you want to be neurotic, you could say, why me? Why, they didn't care about me? Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or they gave me away and not the others? You know? But that wasn't really the case. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, my question was really why do you think she took one of your parents' children in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was really no time. I don't know. She-- at one time, she might have wanted to take some-- another one. But there was-- it was so fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no opportunity, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as it is, she had plenty of trouble with me. You know? In other words, you know, going from place to place--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not for pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever able to get documentation and you know, birth certificates and stuff like that, to show who you are, really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nm-mm. No. In fact, my birth date is really made up.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know when your birth date is? Who made it up for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [? Greenbergs. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Going to show some of your pictures. I think we have a few minutes on this tape, to start showing the pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, these are pictures of my parents. My mother, Esther Leviatin, and Joseph Leviatin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know  where they were taken?

words.text: do you know  where they were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: These pictures were taken in Dubno. And how I got them is through people that were from Dubno, [? Lanzmann. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get them in this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did get them in the United States, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Have you any idea when they were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is-- is of Lena Duzinska, the woman who saved me. And the younger child is my sister, Bayla. And on the right is me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember it being taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe Lena sent this to me after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she had it all along?

speaker: interviewee
text: Apparently.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old do you think you are in that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. What do I look like, four or something? Four? Five, perhaps. These two pictures are of Lena. And the white-and-black picture on the right, we have Lena and her sister, [? Anya, ?] with whom we lived at the end of the war. [? Anya's ?] daughter, and her daughter's daughter. And these pictures were taken about 10 years ago, by someone who went to visit Chervonoarmiyska.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's in-- in the USSR now.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- now it's the Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's Russia. It's the Ukraine. Mhm. And they were just sent to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She brought it back. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is my older sister. Her name was Pearl. I don't recall how I got the photograph. I believe it was sent to the United States to an aunt. And she gave it to me. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture was taken just before the war ended, in Chervonoarmiyska. With me is one of Lena's nieces. I don't recall her name. But on the right is me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know who took the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just don't remember. Lena might have sent it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I just don't remember exactly how it came into my possession.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is a group of children in a-- a DP camp called Bad Reichenhall. And we all joined a-- a kibbutz called [? Yichud ?]. And I am on the left of the flag, sitting down.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe this was in 1947 or '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This I got in the camp, in Bad Reichenhall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- was it a special occasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, getting together, a group.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken of me at Prien am Chiemsee, in Germany, at the [? Strand ?] Hotel, which was an orphanage for children of the survivors that were eventually sent to the United States. And some went to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know who took the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a photograph of a group of orphans taken in Prien, Germany. I am sitting in the second row, to the left of the center.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which girl are you? The third girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The-- should I point to it?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, you can't.

speaker: interviewee
text: No?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. In. We'd like you to introduce your family to the Shoah Foundation interview, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is a part of my family. To my right is my wonderful husband, Jerry. And to my left is my daughter, Pamela Beth Kaidanow. She's my youngest. I have two other children that are not present at the moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Thanks. I wanted just to ask you, Pamela, what kind of impact it had been on you growing up in a family where your mother had the kinds of experiences she did during the Shoah?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that without thinking about it, it impacts me every day, growing up and today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it's-- it's part of who I am, as much as part of who they are. It's easy to forget now. Or not forget, but put in the back of your head now, what they went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And just go through every day like they didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know, when you think about it, you know, it breaks your heart over and over again, every time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, very much so.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't really ever remember not being aware of it. I think it's-- if you can find a blessing in any of it, I think it's made me who I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think I'm all right. And I thank them for it, both my parents. And you know, I thank God they survived it and they're here today with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Do you want to add anything, Mr--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I can at this moment. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I thank you very much, Mrs. Kaidanow, for sharing your story with the Shoah Foundation, and with all of us. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And thank you for being very understanding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.
